title: The box and the ring
checkLabels.t8: The image is in the group
checkLabels.t9: The heading is in the group
checkLabels.t10: The price is in the group
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted everything, the listings included.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
hint2.untouched: The task is to wrap each listing — image, heading and price — in one tag.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed:
Every
wants a
. Count the open ones and the closed ones — the numbers must match.
why.lost: You deleted part of the listings. The task was to group them, not remove them. Bring back the images, the headings and the prices — then wrap them.
why.no-div: There is still no group. The image, the heading and the price are three separate things — but they are one bike. Wrap them.
hint2.no-div: Look at the body. Each listing is three lines stacked: img, h2, p. You need one tag around the three. And there are two listings.
hint3.no-div:
A similar case — two people, each with a name and a job:

Maria

Chef

. The name and the job are now one thing.
hint4.no-div: Open a <div> right before the first image. Close it after the price below it. Then do the same for the second listing.
why.no-class: The group is there, but it is nameless. <div> means nothing on its own — all its meaning comes from the label you give it.
hint2.no-class: class is an attribute. It goes inside the opening tag, not on its own: <div class="...">.
hint3.no-class: Example: <div class="person">. The word in the quotes is yours, but it must say what the box IS.
hint4.no-class: Give it a name that says what the thing inside is — listing, bike, card. Not what it looks like. Empty quotes do not count.
why.outside: Part of the listing is left outside. The image, the heading and the price must all three be INSIDE one <div>.
hint2.outside: Check where you close the . Close it before the price and the price stays out of the box.
hint3.outside:
Here is the mistake:

Maria

Chef

. The job is outside. The box holds only the name.
hint4.outside: The opening tag goes above the image, the closing tag below the price. Everything between them is inside.
blocks.0.text: Two listings. Each is an image, a heading and a price. To the browser these are six independent things, stacked one under the other.
blocks.1.text: Tomorrow you will want each listing in a frame, with a background, with space around it. But there is nothing to grab — there is no "listing", there are three separate tags.
blocks.4.text: <div> is a box. It wraps several things and makes them one.
blocks.5.text: Now there is something to grab. You put a frame on the box and everything inside is framed.
blocks.8.text: <span> is the same, but inline — it lives inside the line and does not break it. It wraps part of a sentence.
blocks.9.text:
Remember and

? The same pair:

is block, is inline.
blocks.11.text: Here is the difference from everything so far. <h1> means heading. <ul> means list. <nav> means menu.
blocks.12.text: <div> means NOTHING. <span> means nothing. They are empty boxes whose only purpose is to give you something to grab.
blocks.13.text: That is exactly why they are dangerous. It is easy to wrap everything in them and tell yourself "it works". It works — but your code no longer says anything about what the page IS.
blocks.14.text: The rule: use <div> when there is REALLY no tag for what you are building.
blocks.15.text: The next lesson is about what happens when you forget that rule.
blocks.16.text: Your turn. Make each listing one thing.
